title: SEGMENT 4 — Genome organization: gag, pol, env, and the LTRs

lines.text: SEGMENT 4 — Genome organization: gag, pol, env, and the LTRs
Take a retroviral RNA genome and lay it out flat, 5' to 3'.
Even though it's an RNA molecule, it looks structurally a lot like a host mRNA — it has a 5' cap, a 3' poly-A tail, and reads in the same direction as it'll be translated.
That's not an accident.
The retroviral RNA genome has to do double duty: it is the mRNA that ribosomes will translate, and it is also the genome that gets packaged into new virions.
The protein-coding content is organized into three main genes, in this order: gag, pol, env.
That's the same order, in essentially every retrovirus you'll ever meet.
gag — short for group-specific antigen, a historical name — encodes the structural proteins of the particle.

It's translated as a single polyprotein called Pr55-Gag in HIV-1, which after maturation gets cleaved by viral protease into the building blocks: matrix MA, capsid CA, nucleocapsid NC, and a small C-terminal piece called p6.
pol — encodes the enzymes.
Protease PR, reverse transcriptase RT, integrase IN.
Pol is not translated as its own independent polyprotein; instead it's translated as a Gag-Pol fusion, via a clever mechanism called a ribosomal frameshift that we'll cover later.
This is how the virus ensures the right stoichiometry — many Gag proteins for every Pol enzyme.
env — encodes the envelope glycoproteins.

The Env precursor is translated from a separately spliced mRNA, trafficked through the secretory pathway, glycosylated, cleaved into SU and TM, and inserted into the plasma membrane where it gets incorporated into budding virions.
That's the protein content.
Three genes, in order.
Simple retroviruses like murine leukemia virus stop there.
Complex retroviruses like HIV have additional accessory genes — tat, rev, nef, vif, vpr, vpu in HIV-1, and we'll meet several of those — but the gag-pol-env spine is universal.
Now, flanking those genes are the most important non-coding sequences in retrovirus biology.
The Long Terminal Repeats, the LTRs.
There's one at each end of the integrated DNA provirus, and they are identical in sequence — that's what "long terminal repeat" means, the same sequence repeated at both terminals.
Each LTR has three sub-regions: U3, R, and U5, in that order.
U3 stands for unique-3-prime, U5 for unique-5-prime, and R for repeated.

The reason for those names is something we'll see clearly when we walk through reverse transcription, but for now just remember: each LTR is U3-R-U5, and the LTRs are the regulatory powerhouse of the provirus.
U3 contains the promoter and enhancer elements that drive transcription of the entire viral genome.
R contains the transcription start and the polyadenylation signal.
U5 contains sequences important for early reverse-transcription chemistry.

So when the integrated provirus is sitting in a host chromosome, transcription initiates at the boundary between U3 and R in the upstream LTR, runs through the whole provirus, and ends at the boundary between R and U5 in the downstream LTR.
The RNA that gets produced therefore starts with R, ends with R, and contains R-U5-gag-pol-env-U3-R, with a cap and a poly-A.
That same RNA can be translated by ribosomes or packaged into new virions.
Now, in addition to the LTRs, there are several short cis-acting sequences scattered through the genome that matter enormously for the life cycle.
Let me name them, because they'll come up again.
The PBS — primer binding site — is a short sequence just downstream of the 5' LTR that is complementary to the 3' end of a specific cellular tRNA.
The virus packages that tRNA, and uses it as the primer for reverse transcription.
For HIV-1, the primer tRNA is tRNA-Lys-3.
The PPT — polypurine tract — is a short adenine- and guanine-rich sequence just upstream of the 3' LTR.
It is the primer for plus-strand DNA synthesis.
We'll see how that works in segment 6.

The psi sequence, also written ψ, is a packaging signal — a structured RNA element near the 5' end of the genome that is recognized by the nucleocapsid domain of Gag, telling the assembling virion "this is a genomic RNA, package me.
" Without psi, an RNA gets translated but not packaged.
Gene therapy vectors are built around removing the viral genes but keeping psi, so the vector RNA still gets packaged.
The att sites at the ends of the LTRs are short sequences recognized by integrase during integration.
Memorize those names.
PBS, PPT, psi, att.
You'll see them again.
